topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the printed memo, the ink blurring slightly under her gaze. Her hand, which had been gripping the folder so tightly it ached, slowly lowered to her side. The fluorescent lights of the office seemed suddenly too bright, the hum of the ventilation system an oppressive drone in the sudden silence of her mind. She reread the line detailing the Senior Manager position, then reread the subsequent line, her breath catching in her throat. A small, involuntary sound escaped her—a soft, choked gasp. She blinked several times, forcing her eyes to focus on the names listed. It was Mark’s name, listed first. A strange, dizzying feeling washed over her, like the floor tilting beneath her feet. She felt a sudden, sharp need to check the document again, as if the information might be faulty, yet her fingers remained frozen above the paper.

[story 2]

"No. No way." Liam muttered under his breath, leaning back in his chair until the worn leather creaked in protest. He picked up the official notification, his fingers trembling slightly as he scanned the qualifications section. His jaw went slack. He slowly lowered the paper, letting it rest on his desk as if it were suddenly too heavy to hold. A flush crept up his neck, warm and undeniable. He shifted in his seat, rubbing the back of his neck with both hands, a nervous habit he hadn't indulged in all morning. He looked over at Sarah, who was already smiling broadly, and managed a weak, disbelieving nod, his voice coming out sounding strangely high-pitched.

[story 3]

Maya watched the email thread unfold on her screen, her fingers hovering over the mouse, unable to click anything. The words detailing the proposed leadership role swam before her eyes, refusing to settle into coherent meaning. She leaned forward, resting her elbows on the cool surface of her desk, her posture rigid. A slow, almost imperceptible shake ran through her shoulders. She cleared her throat, the sound dry and brittle. "This can't be right," she whispered, the disbelief heavy in the tone of her voice. She stared at the screen, tracing the names with her eyes, her gaze lingering on the other candidate’s entry, a profound sense of disorientation settling deep in her chest.
